ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Ayam Geprek. FOODON_03315595 (fried chicken dish) is the closest defensible anchor. The smashed-chicken-with-sambal preparation is not individually coded in FoodOn as of 2024.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FJP (Chicken meat, cooked) is the closest defensible anchor. Ayam Geprek as a composite dish with sambal ijo and rice is not individually represented in FoodEx2 as of 2024.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for Ayam Geprek or Indonesian smashed fried chicken dishes as of 2024.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 350 g, comprising a portion of deep-fried smashed chicken coated with green chilli sambal served alongside steamed white rice.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Chicken, broilers or fryers, meat and skin, cooked, fried, batter (SR Legacy) and Rice, white, long-grain, cooked (SR Legacy); composite estimate for Ayam Geprek with sambal ijo
nutrient_source.organization: U.S. Department of Agriculture, Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for Ayam Geprek with sambal ijo. Nutrient values are estimated from a weighted composite of USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries: batter-fried chicken (FDC ID 171477, approximately 50% by weight), cooked white rice (FDC ID 169756, approximately 35% by weight), and green chilli sambal ingredients including green chilli, shallots, garlic, tomato, and oil (approximately 15% by weight). The input record values (energy 214 kcal, protein 12.86 g, fat 11.43 g, carbohydrate 14.29 g per 100 g) are broadly consistent with this composite and are retained with medium confidence. Sodium at 342.86 mg/100 g reflects added salt in batter and sambal preparation. Singapore HPB Foodo database was consulted but does not contain a direct entry for Ayam Geprek as of 2024.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; and University of Sydney GI Database analogous estimate for mixed fried chicken and white rice dish
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Ayam Geprek with sambal ijo. GI estimated at 65 (medium) based on the dominant carbohydrate source being white rice (GI ~72 per University of Sydney GI Database, entry for white rice, boiled) weighted against the lower-GI contribution of the fried chicken and sambal components. The composite dish GI is estimated at approximately 65, reflecting the dilution of white rice GI by the protein and fat content of the fried chicken. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI 65 × carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (65 × 50.0 g carbohydrate in 350 g serving) / 100 = 32.5. Carbohydrate per serving = 14.29 g/100 g × 350 g = 50.0 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 342.86 mg per 100 g; a 350 g serving delivers approximately 1200 mg sodium, representing over 50% of the WHO recommended daily limit of 2000 mg
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should limit portion size to no more than 150–200 g, request reduced salt in the chicken batter and sambal, and increase vegetable accompaniments to boost potassium intake and partially offset the sodium load.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: White rice is the dominant carbohydrate source with an estimated GI of 65 (medium) and a high glycemic load of 32.5 per standard 350 g serving; dietary fibre at 1.14 g/100 g is insufficient to meaningfully blunt postprandial glucose excursions
type2_diabetes.guidance: Patients with type 2 diabetes should substitute white rice with brown rice or cauliflower rice to reduce glycaemic impact, limit the rice portion to 100–150 g, and increase the proportion of green chilli sambal vegetables to improve fibre intake and slow glucose absorption.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Deep-frying generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) and contributes saturated fat (2.86 g/100 g) and trans fat (0.14 g/100 g); cholesterol at 42.86 mg/100 g and high sodium further elevate cardiovascular risk
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should avoid or strictly limit this dish due to the combination of deep-fried preparation, saturated and trans fat content, high sodium, and cholesterol; if consumed, grilled chicken substituted for fried chicken and a reduced-sodium sambal preparation are strongly recommended.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Deep-frying generates pro-inflammatory AGEs; green chilli and tomato are nightshade ingredients that may exacerbate joint inflammation in sensitive individuals
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid deep-fried preparations due to AGE formation and monitor for joint symptom flares associated with nightshade ingredients (green chilli, tomato); grilled chicken with a reduced nightshade sambal is a preferable alternative.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Ayam Geprek with Fragrant Green Chilli Sambal (Spicy Level 3) is an Indonesian-origin dish widely popular in Singapore, consisting of crispy deep-fried battered chicken that is smashed or pounded and then coated with a vibrant, aromatic green chilli sambal (sambal ijo). The sambal ijo is made from Indonesian green chilies (cabai hijau), shallots, garlic, tomato, lemongrass, and kaffir lime leaves, delivering a fresh, herbaceous, and moderately spicy flavour profile at heat level 3. The dish is typically served with steamed white rice, making it a complete mixed rice meal. The chicken is characterised by a crispy, crunchy exterior from the batter frying and a juicy interior, while the green sambal provides a bright, tangy, and fragrant counterpoint. Per 100 g, the dish provides approximately 214 kcal, 12.86 g protein, 11.43 g fat, and 14.29 g carbohydrate, with sodium at 342.86 mg reflecting the salted batter and sambal seasoning. Vitamin C at 8.57 mg/100 g is contributed by the green chilli. Clinically, the dish is high concern for hypertension due to sodium load (~1200 mg per 350 g serving), high concern for type 2 diabetes due to white rice as the dominant carbohydrate with a medium GI (~65) and high glycaemic load (~32.5 per serving), and high concern for cardiovascular disease due to deep-frying, saturated fat, trans fat, and cholesterol content. The dish is halal-certified in most Singaporean outlets and is a staple of Indonesian-influenced food courts and casual dining establishments across Singapore.

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for Ayam Geprek with sambal ijo in the Singapore HPB Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central. Nutrient values are estimated from a weighted composite of USDA SR Legacy entries for batter-fried chicken and cooked white rice, with sambal ijo ingredients contributing minor nutrient adjustments. The input record values are broadly consistent with this composite and are retained. GI is an analogous estimate based on white rice GI from the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is therefore medium.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for this composite Indonesian dish is not available in USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy or Singapore HPB Foodo database as of 2024. 2. potassium_mg is set to -1: potassium data for this composite dish is not individually reported in the consulted approved sources. 3. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: micronutrient and moisture data for this specific composite dish preparation are not available in the consulted approved sources (USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy, Singapore HPB Foodo) as of 2024. 4. Nutrient values are estimated from a USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy composite (batter-fried chicken ~50%, cooked white rice ~35%, sambal ijo ingredients ~15% by weight). 5. The input source URL (https://foodo.sg/) was consulted but does not contain a direct Ayam Geprek entry; the HPB Singapore Foodo database was therefore used only for contextual reference and USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy is the primary nutrient evidence source. 6. trans_fat_g at 0.14 g/100 g is retained from the input record, consistent with trace trans fat from deep-frying in partially hydrogenated oils; this is a plausible value for deep-fried battered chicken. 7. This dish is Indonesian in origin but is widely served in Singapore; multilingual names are provided in Chinese (simplified and traditional), Malay, Tamil, and Hindi to support Singaporean food database retrieval in addition to the mandatory Indonesian local name.
